title: A Guide To Stablecoins: Multi-Collateral-Backed Stablecoins—DAI, USDS
gist: ARK's deep dive on Sky Protocol's DAI/USDS shows the category lost more than half its stablecoin market share since 2021 (7% to 4%) due to capital-inefficient overcollateralization, and is now pivoting toward RWA-backed, Agent-deployed reserves and a two-tier compliance strategy (immutable DAI vs. upgradeable, freezable USDS) to stay relevant post-GENIUS Act.

insights.claim: Multi-collateral-backed stablecoins (the original crypto-native design) peaked at ~7% of total stablecoin market share in late 2021 and have fallen to ~4%, having never hit escape velocity because of capital inefficiencies from overcollateralized vaults.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: The category's structural growth ceiling versus fiat-backed stablecoins (USDT/USDC) means Sky's re-architecture toward RWAs is a survival strategy, not just optimization — track whether market share stabilizes or keeps eroding.
insights.quote: multi-collateral-backed stablecoins peaked in late 2021 at ~7% of the stablecoin market and have dropped to ~4% market share

insights.claim: Because multi-collateral stablecoins like DAI and USDS do not meet the GENIUS Act's definition of a 'payments stablecoin,' they face a structural regulatory disadvantage versus majority fiat-backed competitors, pushing Sky Protocol to compete instead on reserve diversification, RWAs, and tokenization.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: Regulatory exclusion forces a differentiated strategy (yield-bearing, RWA-backed) rather than head-on payments competition with USDC/USDT — a durable niche if RWA tokenization keeps growing.

insights.claim: Sky Protocol, the largest multi-collateral issuer, has transitioned roughly half its stablecoin supply away from the legacy overcollateralized model toward new strategic objectives (RWAs, agent-deployed capital), positioning it as the dominant force behind the category's evolution.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: Sky is effectively a bellwether for whether crypto-native stablecoins can escape the capital-inefficiency trap; its migration pace is a proxy for category health.

themes.theme: Shared architecture obscures true risk allocation

insights.claim: DAI and USDS are both merely ERC-20 wrappers around a single shared internal ledger (the vat.dai contract), meaning there is no distinction between which specific reserves back DAI versus which back USDS — the backing is verifiable only in aggregate, never per-token.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: Investors can't isolate DAI-specific or USDS-specific collateral risk — any protocol-level shock (RWA default, USDC freeze) hits both tokens equally regardless of which one they hold.
insights.quote: backing is verifiable only in aggregate

insights.claim: Because both stablecoins interface with the same internal balance system, all systemic risks within Sky Protocol — collateral seizure, governance mismanagement, or reckless DeFi exposure — impact DAI and USDS equally, regardless of each token's own governance/compliance design differences.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: The perceived safety difference between 'compliant USDS' and 'decentralized DAI' is cosmetic at the solvency level — a real depeg event would hit holders of both identically.

insights.claim: Only ~12% of DAI/USDS backing now comes from the original Core CDP Vault model that once served as the primary peg-stability mechanism via arbitrage; the peg is now stabilized mainly through protocol-controlled liquidity (LitePSM, SLL, Staking Engine) rather than market-driven arbitrage.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: The system has quietly shifted from a decentralized arbitrage-based peg mechanism to one dependent on centralized-style liquidity management and USDC — a meaningful decentralization/censorship-resistance regression worth flagging to DeFi-purist investors.

themes.theme: Reserve composition and the rise of 'Agents'

insights.claim: Sky Protocol's reserves are dominated by Stablecoins (~30%, mostly USDC in the LitePSM) and the Spark Liquidity Layer (~28%), with the remaining ~42% spread across Core CDP Vaults, Grove, the Staking Engine, Legacy RWAs, and newer initiatives like Obex.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: Roughly 30% of the reserve base backing a 'decentralized' stablecoin is itself USDC — DAI/USDS solvency is materially linked to Circle's USDC solvency and any USDC freeze/depeg event.

insights.claim: Semi-independent capital allocators called 'Agents' (formerly 'Stars') — including Spark, Grove, and Obex — borrow USDS from Sky at a set base rate and deploy it into governance-approved yield strategies, capturing the spread themselves while Sky earns revenue at the lending layer and pushes allocation risk outward.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: This is effectively an internal shadow-banking layer inside a stablecoin protocol — a fractional/rehypothecation-style structure whose credit quality depends on Agent-level underwriting discipline that isn't directly visible to DAI/USDS holders.

insights.claim: Grove, one of Sky's Agents, enables USDS issuance backed by tokenized private credit and offchain lending instruments — meaning a portion of USDS's backing is effectively exposure to offchain, non-crypto private credit risk.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: USDS holders carry indirect exposure to private-credit default risk, a factor absent from purely crypto-collateralized stablecoins — worth monitoring as private credit markets face stress.

insights.claim: The Spark Liquidity Layer deploys reserves into external DeFi protocols — primarily SparkLend, Morpho, Curve, and Maple — meaning a meaningful share of DAI/USDS backing is actively re-deployed into third-party lending/liquidity protocols rather than held idle.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: A cascading failure or exploit in Morpho, Curve, or Maple could directly impair Sky's reserve base — cross-protocol contagion risk that's easy to overlook when just checking DAI/USDS's own contract.

insights.claim: Coinbase Prime reportedly serves as the custody and MPC provider for the account holding the USDC reserves that back the LitePSM, even though it does not operate or govern the module itself.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: A supposedly decentralized stablecoin system has a concentrated, named centralized custodian in its critical peg-stabilization mechanism — a single point of operational/counterparty risk investors should weight.

themes.theme: Insolvency waterfall and quantified capital cushion

insights.claim: System-wide Agent collateral sits at ~$13.9 billion backing ~$11.9 billion of issued stablecoin debt, leaving Agents roughly 116% overcollateralized as the first line of defense against protocol losses.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable solvency ratio — a drop meaningfully below ~116% would be an early warning signal for depeg risk well before a Backstop Event triggers.
insights.quote: system-wide agent collateral sits at ~$13.9 billion backing ~$11.9 billion of debt (issued stablecoins), leaving agents ~116% overcollateralized

insights.claim: Sky's Aggregate Backstop Capital (ABC) — the second loss-absorption layer — nominally totals ~$100.84 million in Genesis Capital across Agent treasuries, but because the protocol's Surplus Buffer is currently negative at around -$34 million, the true usable backstop is only about $66 million.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: The headline backstop figure overstates real loss-absorbing capacity by roughly 35% once the negative operational Surplus Buffer is netted out — investors relying on the gross Genesis Capital number would misjudge protocol resilience.
insights.quote: The Surplus Buffer sits at around ~-$34 million today, leaving ~$66m as the true backstop available for recovery within the ABC

insights.claim: If losses exceed all available Aggregate Backstop Capital, Sky triggers a 'Backstop Event' that automatically mints and sells SKY tokens on the open market to recapitalize the system; this mint is normally capped but can be overridden to become uncapped in a true emergency, and if still insufficient, the USDS target price is adjusted below $1 to settle remaining bad debt.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: This is a dilution-based bailout mechanism (like an equity issuance) that would crush SKY token holders in a severe tail event, and as a last resort explicitly permits an official USDS depeg below $1 rather than an all-out default.

insights.claim: Cross-chain DAI/USDS holdings outside Ethereum are not tracked by Sky, so losses from a bridge failure or compromise on another chain are isolated to that chain — Ethereum-based reserves remain unaffected and any bailout intervention for those bridged assets is optional, not guaranteed.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: Holders of bridged DAI/USDS on non-Ethereum chains bear uninsured bridge risk that mainnet holders do not — a material distinction glossed over by treating DAI/USDS as fungible across chains.

themes.theme: Governance concentration and the DAI/USDS control asymmetry

insights.claim: Two Aligned Delegates — 'cloaky' and 'Blue' — control more than 75% of delegated SKY voting power, giving them supermajority ability to pass or block almost any governance vote, a concentration level that persists only as long as those delegates keep acting in delegators' interests.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: Governance is effectively controlled by two entities rather than being broadly decentralized — a key centralization/counterparty risk for anyone treating Sky governance as trustlessly diffuse; watch for redelegation events as an early signal of governance stress.
insights.quote: two ADs—cloaky and Blue—account for more than 75% of delegated SKY, giving them supermajority control over governance decisions

insights.claim: DAI's ERC-20 contract was deliberately designed to be immutable (confirmed by founder Rune Christensen), making it immune to governance-forced upgrades or deprecation, whereas the USDS contract was designed to be upgradeable with community approval — meaning Sky governance can add mint/burn logic or freeze/blacklist functionality to USDS but can never alter existing DAI.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: This is the single clearest structural fork between the two tokens: DAI is a permanent decentralization commitment while USDS is a governance-controllable instrument with latent freeze/blacklist capability — investors optimizing for censorship resistance should prefer DAI, those wanting compliance optionality should prefer USDS.

insights.claim: The USDS contract already contains upgradability hooks that support address freezing, even though that functionality is not currently active — activation would require only a governance vote, not a new contract deployment.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: USDS's censorship-resistance is not a hard technical guarantee but a soft governance policy choice that could flip via a single vote — materially different risk than DAI's hard-coded immutability.
insights.quote: the USDS contract includes upgradability hooks that support freezing, subject to a governance vote

insights.claim: Even though Sky Protocol cannot freeze, blacklist, or seize DAI directly, most of DAI's collateral (fiat-backed stablecoins, RWAs, protocol-owned liquidity) is itself subject to freezes/censorship at the asset level — so DAI faces meaningful indirect censorship risk despite its contract-level immutability.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: DAI's 'censorship resistant' reputation is overstated in practice — a freeze of underlying USDC/RWA collateral could impair DAI's backing and peg even though the DAI token itself can't be touched, a subtlety many investors miss.

themes.theme: Liquidity migration reshaping DAI vs USDS

insights.claim: Despite launching less than two years ago, USDS has already surpassed DAI in onchain liquidity, driven by a wave of centralized-exchange migrations in early-to-mid 2026 in which major exchanges automatically converted user DAI balances 1:1 into USDS and delisted DAI outright.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: DAI is being pushed out of centralized-exchange rails and is increasingly a DeFi-only, niche instrument, while USDS becomes Sky's flagship product across both CeFi and DeFi — a structural liquidity shift investors should factor into which token has better exit liquidity.

insights.claim: Both DAI and USDS remain significantly smaller than the majority fiat-backed leaders USDT and USDC, and have each crossed $1 billion in supply on only Ethereum.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: The multi-collateral category's scale ceiling relative to fiat-backed stablecoins reinforces that DAI/USDS are a specialized DeFi/RWA niche, not payments-scale competitors.

insights.claim: Native minting and redemption of DAI/USDS can occur only on Ethereum mainnet, where all vault contracts, protocol logic, and underlying collateral reside — even though the tokens circulate across other EVM chains and Solana via bridges like Wormhole's NTT.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: All bridged/multichain DAI and USDS supply is a derivative claim dependent on bridge integrity, not a native asset — a key risk layer for anyone holding these tokens outside Ethereum mainnet.

themes.theme: Yield mechanics as a GENIUS Act workaround

insights.claim: Although DAI and USDS in base form are not marketed as 'yield-bearing,' Sky Protocol generates revenue from RWA and SLL collateral plus borrower stability fees, and channels a growing share of that revenue back to holders via staking derivatives — sDAI (Dai Savings Rate) and sUSDS (Sky Savings Rate) — letting holders earn protocol yield while still deploying the tokens across DeFi.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: This yield-pass-through is a genuine product differentiator versus GENIUS Act-compliant payment stablecoins (USDC/USDT), which by law cannot pay yield directly to holders — a durable regulatory arbitrage for Sky as long as DAI/USDS stay outside the Act's scope.
insights.quote: USDS demonstrates how this design can pass protocol revenue back to holders through the Sky Savings Rate, offering a differentiated, natively yield-bearing instrument that GENIUS Act payments stablecoins cannot replicate

insights.claim: A growing share of Sky's revenue comes from lending USDS to Agents at a set base rate, who then must earn a spread above that borrowing cost by deploying it into yield strategies — pushing allocation and credit-risk decisions outward to semi-independent operators while Sky captures a lending-layer margin.
insights.speaker: Raye Hadi
insights.so_what: This is structurally similar to a bank's net interest margin model built on top of a stablecoin — Sky's revenue growth is now tied to Agent risk appetite and yield-strategy performance rather than simple collateral custody.
